title: Make Transportation Authorities Accountable and Transparent Act
short_title: Make Transportation Authorities Accountable and Transparent Act
stage_label: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.
plain_summary: This bill tells a government watchdog office to check how the five biggest public transportation systems in the country spent their federal money over the last five years. The watchdog must look at all the money these transit agencies got from the government, including special coronavirus relief money. After they finish checking the spending, the watchdog office must write a report about what they found and give it to Congress within six months.
last_action_text: Referred to the Subcommittee on Highways and Transit.

sections.title: Section 1  Short Title
sections.text: This law can be called the Make Transportation Authorities Accountable and Transparent Act.

sections.title: Section 2  Inspector General Audit on Public Transportation Coronavirus Relief Spending
sections.text: The inspector general (a government watchdog who checks how money is spent) of the Department of Transportation must check how certain public transportation agencies used federal money during the five years before this law was passed. The inspector general must look at the five biggest public transportation agencies in the country. These are the agencies that had the most individual passenger trips in two thousand nineteen. The audit (detailed money check) must show how much money each agency got from federal coronavirus relief laws and regular transportation funding. It must also explain exactly how they spent that money. The inspector general has one hundred eighty days after this law passes to finish the audit and send a report to Congress. The laws being checked include regular transportation funding laws and several coronavirus relief laws passed in two thousand twenty and two thousand twenty-one. These include the Coronavirus Preparedness and Response Supplemental Appropriations Act from two thousand twenty, the Coronavirus Aid Relief and Economic Security Act, the Consolidated Appropriations Act from two thousand twenty-one, and the American Rescue Plan Act from two thousand twenty-one.
